attributes.stem:
An eight-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with his parents due to a nosebleed that developed while playing with his father, who accidentally hit his elbow on the boy's nose. The parents tried to stop the bleeding by pinching the nose for 30 minutes without success. The mother reports that her son had a fever and a "cold" one week ago but has been well since. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 150/min, blood pressure 70/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Physical examination reveals several small pinpoint bruises over the back and both shins.

Investigations reveal:

  • Haemoglobin 125 g/L (115-165)
  • White cell count 9.1 x 10^9/L (3.6-11.0)
  • Platelets 15 x 10^9/L (140-400)
  • Prothrombin time 15 seconds
  • Activated partial thromboplastin time 36 seconds

A peripheral blood smear reveals normal platelet morphology.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management, in addition to local measures to control the nasal bleeding?
options: IVIG plus corticosteroids plus platelet transfusion
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with signs and symptoms consistent with immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The key features include unrelenting nosebleed, recent viral illness, scattered ecchymosis, and isolated low platelet count. ITP is an autoimmune condition characterized by the formation of IgG autoantibodies against platelets, leading to thrombocytopenia and mucosal bleeding.

IVIG plus corticosteroids plus platelet transfusion is the most appropriate initial management for this patient. The child is haemodynamically unstable (hypotensive and tachycardic) with uncontrolled bleeding, indicating a need for urgent intervention. IVIG and corticosteroids are first-line treatments for ITP, working to reduce autoantibody-mediated platelet destruction. In severe cases with life-threatening bleeding, platelet transfusion may be necessary, although it's generally avoided in ITP due to rapid destruction of transfused platelets by autoantibodies. However, when given in conjunction with IVIG and corticosteroids, platelet transfusion can help to temporarily increase platelet counts and control severe bleeding.

Romiplostim is a thrombopoietin receptor agonist that stimulates platelet production. While it can be effective in ITP, it's typically reserved for cases that have failed other treatments and is not appropriate as initial management in an acute, severe presentation.

Observation alone would be appropriate for asymptomatic ITP patients with only cutaneous symptoms. However, this patient has uncontrolled bleeding and haemodynamic instability, requiring active intervention.

Platelet transfusion alone is generally not recommended in ITP as the transfused platelets are likely to be rapidly destroyed by the existing autoantibodies. When necessary, platelet transfusion should be given in conjunction with IVIG and corticosteroids to minimize destruction.

Splenectomy is considered a third-line treatment for ITP in Australia, typically reserved for patients who have failed first-line (corticosteroids, IVIG) and second-line (further immunosuppression, e.g., rituximab) treatments. It's usually deferred for at least 12 months after diagnosis, as many patients undergo spontaneous remission.

In summary, the combination of IVIG, corticosteroids, and platelet transfusion is the most appropriate initial management for this haemodynamically unstable child with severe ITP and uncontrolled bleeding. This approach aims to rapidly increase platelet count, reduce autoantibody-mediated platelet destruction, and control the life-threatening haemorrhage.
learningPoints: Immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) is characterized by scattered petechiae and mucosal bleeding after trauma. It is usually a benign condition developing after a viral illness and requires just observation. Active management is required if there is uncontrolled bleeding or when the platelet count is <20 x 109/L in adults.
IVIG plus steroids are the treatment of choice in a haemodynamically unstable patient with ITP.
Platelet transfusion may be necessary in severe ITP with life-threatening bleeding, but should be given in conjunction with IVIG and corticosteroids.
references: Provan D, Arnold DM, Bussel JB, Chong BH, Cooper N, Gernsheimer T, Ghanima W, Godeau B, González-López TJ, Grainger J, Hou M, Kruse C, McDonald V, Michel M, Newland AC, Pavord S, Rodeghiero F, Scully M, Tomiyama Y, Wong RS, Zaja F, Kuter DJ. Updated international consensus report on the investigation and management of primary immune thrombocytopenia. Blood Adv. 2019 Nov 26;3(22):3780-3817.
